concept: Throw / Send Through the Air / Release into Flight
description: To throw: a stone into the pond, seed across the furrow, the rope to the boat. Sending a thing through the air by release. With 'pilu' (take) and 'loa' (give) it completes the hand's grammar; the sower and the child at the pond own this word, not the combatant.
sound_symbolism: 'wa' swings outward and 'pho' is the breath-burst of release — the word is an arm's arc ending in letting go.
grammatical_notes: Transitive with target marked: 'lopia muo melothe kerou wapho.' (The child throws a stone into the pond.) 'mia wei thia suro wapho.' (I throw the rope to you.) Sowing: 'lo mia thinoe wapho.' (We throw seed.)

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The word's examples keep the arc innocent: stones to ponds, ropes to boats, seed to soil. What a language shows thrown is what its speakers first imagine.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Broadcast sowing is throwing done with judgment — the oldest agricultural gesture, seed arcing from an open hand.

tags.physical: The arm's arc and letting go
